goal: refactor(hooks): drop semantic auto-flow, keep hooks as context provider

summary.title: Hooks as context provider, not workflow driver
summary.what: Refactor the agent-hooks subsystem so hooks run only mechanical operations (sync, status) and inject the result into the agent's system context at sessionStart, while every other lifecycle event becomes a webhook-only signal. Delete the auto-Start / auto-Append / auto-SealPrepare branches and helpers from the dispatcher; shrink HooksSection / DispatchSettings / EngineFacade accordingly; add an optional HookOutputRenderer interface and implement it on the cursor agent for sessionStart additional_context output; rewrite agents-md / agents-stub templates and README to position hooks as a context provider.
summary.why: The previous design embedded semantic judgment in the hook subprocess: TurnStart used the user's first prompt as an intent goal; TurnEnd auto-Appended a synthesized turn description; SessionEnd auto-SealPrepared. Hooks have no LLM available, so every push / smoke-test / rebase / 'look at X' prompt got minted as an intent goal and silent stop hooks turned into 'agent turn (completed)' turn pollution. The mainline contract in AGENTS.md is explicit that semantic judgment is the agent's responsibility — start, goal text, append, append text, fingerprint, phase-2 verdict are all LLM jobs. The fix is structural: hooks must only do deterministic operations and surface state, never produce semantic content.
summary.user_goal: Fix the procedural-prompt-as-goal regression by redesigning the hooks subsystem from first principles: hooks provide context, the agent makes all decisions.

decisions.point: Where to draw the hook / agent boundary
decisions.chose: Hooks run only mechanical, deterministic operations (sync, status) and may inject context for the agent at sessionStart. All semantic judgment stays with the agent.
decisions.rationale: Hooks are subprocesses without LLM intelligence. Any rule a hook might use for semantic judgment (keyword blacklist, prompt-shape heuristic, summary fallback) is a worse approximation of agent reasoning than the agent itself. The boundary is enforced by narrowing EngineFacade to {Sync, Status} so adding a semantic shortcut requires widening the surface — a conversation we want to have explicitly.
rejected: Keyword/phrase blacklist on prompts to skip auto-start (the original 'fix'). Rejected as a 'garbage solution' by the user: it pretends to do semantic judgment without LLM and just relocates the problem.
Prompt-shape heuristics (length, punctuation, code-block presence). Same root issue.
Auto-start with a placeholder goal that the agent renames later. Pollutes mainline log with placeholder titles and adds a new failure mode (rename forgotten).

decisions.point: How to deliver the sessionStart snapshot to the agent
decisions.chose: Cursor's third-party-hooks protocol allows sessionStart hooks to return {"continue": true, "additional_context": "<markdown>"} which gets prepended to the agent's system context. Use that for cursor; provide a generic HookOutputRenderer interface so other agents can opt in to their own protocol shape.
decisions.rationale: sessionStart is the only hook event in cursor's protocol that can push state into the agent's reasoning. beforeSubmitPrompt / stop / subagentStop / sessionEnd cannot. Investing in sessionStart gives the agent the determinstic state up front; the agent then drives the rest from AGENTS.md.
rejected: Per-prompt fresh status injection at every beforeSubmitPrompt. Cursor protocol does not support context injection on that event.

Stuff sessionStart additional_context with the top-N relevant intents from `mainline log`. Rejected: ranking is a semantic task and would re-introduce the same anti-pattern. Instead, the hint tells the agent to call `mainline log` itself.

decisions.point: What the sessionStart markdown should contain
decisions.chose: Three neutral sections: status JSON snapshot, sync summary (or sync error), and a scenario hint pointing at AGENTS.md plus the two CLI calls (mainline log / mainline show) the agent should run before non-trivial work.
decisions.rationale: The markdown is consumed as system context by the LLM, so it must not produce content the agent should produce itself (goal text, append text, fingerprint). Limiting it to deterministic state + pointers preserves the agent as the sole source of semantic content.

decisions.point: Whether to retain AutoSealPrepare on SessionEnd
decisions.chose: Drop it entirely. SessionEnd becomes a webhook-only signal.
decisions.rationale: seal --prepare's value is staging an evidence package the agent then turns into a SealResult. Doing it on SessionEnd without the agent in the loop creates a stale snapshot the agent has to redo or reconcile next session. The agent already runs seal --prepare deliberately when its task is done per AGENTS.md.

decisions.chose: Cache the error on the dispatcher and include it in the rendered markdown ('Sync FAILED: <err>; treat snapshot as potentially stale'). Continue rendering status from the locally-known view.
decisions.rationale: Network being down on session start should NEVER block the agent. Surfacing the failure inside additional_context keeps the agent informed without crashing the session.

rejected.alternative: Add a `Settings.SkipProceduralPrompts` boolean with a phrase blacklist
rejected.reason: Patches the symptom by encoding bad heuristics in Go. Fails the design principle that semantic decisions belong to the agent.
Cursor protocol does not allow context injection on that event.

rejected.alternative: Pre-rank related intents and embed them in additional_context
rejected.reason: Ranking is a semantic task; the hint tells the agent to call `mainline log` itself, preserving the boundary.

risks: Agents that rely on the previous 'auto-flow' may now skip start/append/seal-prepare entirely if their prompt-handling does not honor AGENTS.md. Mitigation: sessionStart additional_context explicitly instructs the agent to follow AGENTS.md and lists the commands it should run.

sessionStart context is one-shot per session; the agent must run `mainline status` again before architectural decisions made later in the session. The hint in additional_context calls this out, but agents that rely on memory may forget.

DispatchSettings / HooksSection field deletion is a config-schema breakage. TOML files that still set auto_start_intent / auto_append_turn / auto_seal_prepare will see those values silently ignored on load (Go's TOML decoder does not error on unknown keys with the current setup). User-visible only if they look at the parsed config; status output no longer prints the fields.
followups: Add an integration test that boots a fake cursor sessionStart payload, runs `mainline hooks cursor session-start`, and asserts the JSON envelope shape and additional_context content (status snapshot block, sync summary block, scenario-hint block).
When Codex / Claude Code agents land, decide their HookOutputRenderer return shape (each host has its own context-injection contract).
Consider adding a `mainline hooks doctor` command that detects stale config keys (auto_start_intent etc.) in .mainline/config.toml and warns the user.

architectural_claims: Hooks are mechanical, not semantic: they run sync/status and inject deterministic state, never produce content that requires LLM judgment.
Cursor's sessionStart is the only hook event in the protocol that can inject system context into the agent; investment is concentrated there.
EngineFacade is intentionally narrow ({Sync, Status}) so adding hook-side automation requires widening the contract explicitly.
HookOutputRenderer is an OPTIONAL Agent interface: stdout protocol shape lives in each agent package, not in the hooks core.
AGENTS.md remains the single source of truth for the agent contract regardless of whether hooks are installed.
behavioral_changes: TurnStart no longer calls mainline start; the dispatcher emits only a webhook envelope on TurnStart.
TurnEnd / SubagentEnd no longer call mainline append; webhook envelopes only.
SessionEnd no longer calls mainline seal --prepare; webhook envelope only.
SessionStart caches sync result and status on the dispatcher; the cursor agent reads them via LastSync/LastStatus to render additional_context.
Cursor sessionStart now writes JSON ({"continue":true,"additional_context":"..."}) to stdout; previously it wrote nothing.
mainline hooks status no longer reports auto_start_intent / auto_append_turn / auto_seal_prepare; only enabled and auto_sync_on_session_start.
mainline hooks install footer message no longer claims hooks 'auto-run mainline at lifecycle points'; states the context-provider model explicitly.

api_changes.signature: interface hooks.HookOutputRenderer { RenderHookOutput(hookName string, dispatcher *Dispatcher, ev *Event, dispatchResult any) ([]byte, error) }
(cursor.Agent).RenderHookOutput(hookName string, dispatcher *hooks.Dispatcher, ev *hooks.Event, dispatchResult any) ([]byte, error)
type hooks.EngineFacade interface narrowed to {Sync, Status} (was 6 methods)
type engine.HookFacade interface narrowed to {Sync, Status}
type domain.HooksSection — fields AutoStartIntent / AutoAppendTurn / AutoSealPrepare removed
type hooks.DispatchSettings — fields AutoStartIntent / AutoAppendTurn / AutoSealPrepare removed
[hooks] auto_start_intent / auto_append_turn / auto_seal_prepare keys removed; auto_sync_on_session_start retained

migration_notes: Existing .mainline/config.toml entries with auto_start_intent / auto_append_turn / auto_seal_prepare under [hooks] are silently ignored; no migration required. Users who want to clean up can delete those keys.
Cursor users with the previous .cursor/hooks.json wrappers should re-run `mainline hooks install --agent cursor --force` to pick up the new sessionStart wrapper that propagates additional_context.

Webhook subscribers that relied on intent_started / turn_appended / seal_prepared events firing from hooks will no longer receive those events at hook time; they still fire from the engine when the agent invokes the corresponding mainline command.
